title: Myocarditis
oneLiner: Acute inflammation of heart muscle; common in young adults presenting with chest pain, dyspnoea, arrhythmia; can rapidly cause heart failure/cardiogenic shock.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute MI:** Do not assume young age = myocarditis. Single finding: **regional ST elevation with reciprocal changes** on ECG (vs diffuse ST elevation in myocarditis). If any doubt, treat as STEMI – activate primary PCI pathway. Troponin alone cannot differentiate.
- **Cardiac tamponade:** Exudative pericarditis can cause tamponade, especially if myopericarditis. Single finding: **pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg** or bedside echo showing right atrial/ventricular collapse. Requires urgent pericardiocentesis.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Chest pain (often pleuritic, positional, may mimic pericarditis or MI – worse lying flat, relieved sitting forward)
  - Dyspnoea on exertion or at rest
  - Palpitations / presyncope / syncope (from arrhythmia)
  - Prodromal viral illness (fever, myalgia, coryza) in preceding 1–2 weeks
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachycardia out of proportion to fever
  - Muffled heart sounds (if large effusion/tamponade)
  - Pericardial rub (if associated pericarditis – "myopericarditis")
  - Signs of heart failure: raised JVP, peripheral oedema, S3 gallop, bibasal crackles
  - Hypotension ± narrow pulse pressure (suggests low output state)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Acute coronary syndrome (MI)** – regional ECG changes, reciprocal depression, risk factors
  - **Pericarditis** – chest pain but no myocardial involvement (normal troponin, echo normal)
  - **Takotsubo cardiomyopathy** – post-menopausal women, emotional stress, apical ballooning on echo
  - **Viral respiratory infection** – no cardiac signs, troponin normal
  - **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, D-dimer/cTPA needed
- **Splitter:** The **ECG** – diffuse ST elevation (myocarditis/pericarditis) vs regional ST elevation with reciprocal changes (MI). Also, **echo** – segmental wall motion abnormality (MI) vs global dysfunction (myocarditis).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - 12-lead ECG (immediate) – look for diffuse ST elevation, PR depression, low voltage, electrical alternans (tamponade)
  - Capillary blood gas (lactate, pH if shock)
  - Focused echo (if available) – assess LV function, effusion, tamponade
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, troponin (high-sensitivity – often elevated but rises slower than MI)
  - D-dimer (if PE suspected)
  - Viral serology (later – EBV, CMV, parvovirus B19, COVID-19) – not urgent in first hour
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (if heart failure suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First line:** Chest X-ray (cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, effusions)
  - **Second line:** Transthoracic echo (urgent if haemodynamic instability or diagnostic uncertainty) – assess LV size, function, wall motion, effusion
  - **If stable:** Cardiac MRI (with T2-STIR and LGE) – gold standard for non-invasive diagnosis
- **Special tests:**
  - Endomyocardial biopsy (only if unexplained heart failure, giant cell/tuberculosis/sarcoid suspected)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** The **Lake Louise Criteria** (MRI-based – T2 oedema + LGE). Not for acute ward use, but helpful for cardiology referral.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG + troponin** in any young adult with chest pain – missing MI delays reperfusion.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stabilise airway/breathing/circulation.** If shock: IV fluids (but avoid overload if LV dysfunction), inotropes (e.g., dobutamine) – consult ICU
  - **Treat arrhythmias** – if haemodynamically unstable, DC cardioversion; if stable, consider amiodarone (avoid class I agents in acute myocarditis)
  - **Pain control:** Paracetamol or NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) if no contraindications (caution with heart failure/renal impairment). Colchicine can be added for pericarditis component.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Heart failure management** – if LVEF <40%: start ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril) + beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol) after haemodynamic stabilisation
  - **Monitor** – continuous ECG telemetry (risk of ventricular arrhythmias)
  - **Serial echocardiograms** – every 24–48h if unstable
  - **Hydration** – keep euvolemic; avoid excessive fluids
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Restrict physical activity** for at least 3–6 months (risk of sudden cardiac death with exercise)
  - **Follow-up echo** at 3–6 months to assess recovery
  - **Immunosuppression** (e.g., prednisolone) only for specific causes (giant cell, sarcoid, fungal) – discuss with cardiology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 12h if euvolemic (watch for overload). If cardiogenic shock, restrict to 20–30 mL/kg.
  - VTE prophylaxis: Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin) if not bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal (avoid high sodium if heart failure)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (including BP, HR, SpO2, temperature); if telemetry, document any arrhythmia
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** 1 g PO/IV QDS PRN pain (max 4 g/day)
  - **Ibuprofen** 400–600 mg PO TDS (if no heart failure/renal impairment) – max 2.4 g/day
  - **Colchicine** 500 µg PO BD for 3 months (if pericarditis component) – check renal function (reduce if eGFR <30)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Dobutamine** – for cardiogenic shock: start at 2.5 µg/kg/min, titrate to effect (but only in ICU/HDU setting)
  - **Amiodarone** – for VT: 300 mg IV over 20–60 min (contraindicated in hyperthyroidism/long QT)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs – avoid in renal impairment, GI bleed, heart failure
  - Colchicine – reduces clearance if on clarithromycin, statins, cyclosporine; check CYP3A4
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Colchicine: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (500 µg alternate days) – avoid if on dialysis
  - Ramipril: start at 1.25 mg OD if eGFR <30, up-titrate slowly
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **NSAIDs given to patient with undiagnosed heart failure or acute kidney injury** – can precipitate oliguric renal failure or pulmonary oedema. Always check creatinine and clinical volume status first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cardiogenic shock (5–10%)
  - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT/VF) – can be fatal
  - Heart block (especially giant cell or sarcoid)
  - Pericardial effusion / tamponade
- **Chronic:**
  - Dilated cardiomyopathy (persistent LV dysfunction)
  - Chronic heart failure
  - Recurrent myocarditis
  - Sudden cardiac death (rare if full recovery)
sections.Prognosis:
- Most patients recover fully with supportive care (70–80%)
- Mortality <5% (higher if viral/staphylococcal or giant cell)
- Chronic heart failure develops in ~20% – require lifelong monitoring and medication
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO** a **bedside echo** (even limited) within the first hour – look for LV function, effusion, tamponade
- **DO** **start telemetry** immediately – the first 48h are highest risk for arrhythmia
- **DO** **tell the patient to avoid exercise** for 3–6 months; even light exertion can precipitate arrhythmia or worsen inflammation
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NOT** assuming young age = myocarditis – always rule out MI with ECG and troponin
- **NOT** giving **NSAIDs** without checking renal function and heart failure status
- **NOT** discharging without arranging **cardiology follow-up and echo** – recovery may be incomplete and needs monitoring
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Chest pain worsens or becomes unrelieved by simple analgesia
  - New shortness of breath, palpitations, syncope, or dizzy spells
  - Fever returns with worsening malaise
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology outpatient review within 2–4 weeks
  - Repeat echo at 6 weeks and 6 months
  - Advise to inform GP and avoid driving if arrhythmia history (DVLA rules)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or shock (systolic BP <90, lactate >2 mmol/L)
  - ECG shows VT/VF or high-grade AV block (Mobitz II/complete heart block)
  - Echo shows severe LV dysfunction (LVEF <35%) or tamponade
  - New arrhythmia causing haemodynamic instability
- **Crash team:** if any of the above with loss of consciousness or respiratory arrest

rawMarkdown:
## Myocarditis
**One-liner:** Acute inflammation of heart muscle; common in young adults presenting with chest pain, dyspnoea, arrhythmia; can rapidly cause heart failure/cardiogenic shock.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute MI:** Do not assume young age = myocarditis. Single finding: **regional ST elevation with reciprocal changes** on ECG (vs diffuse ST elevation in myocarditis). If any doubt, treat as STEMI – activate primary PCI pathway. Troponin alone cannot differentiate.
- **Cardiac tamponade:** Exudative pericarditis can cause tamponade, especially if myopericarditis. Single finding: **pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg** or bedside echo showing right atrial/ventricular collapse. Requires urgent pericardiocentesis.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Chest pain (often pleuritic, positional, may mimic pericarditis or MI – worse lying flat, relieved sitting forward)
  - Dyspnoea on exertion or at rest
  - Palpitations / presyncope / syncope (from arrhythmia)
  - Prodromal viral illness (fever, myalgia, coryza) in preceding 1–2 weeks
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachycardia out of proportion to fever
  - Muffled heart sounds (if large effusion/tamponade)
  - Pericardial rub (if associated pericarditis – "myopericarditis")
  - Signs of heart failure: raised JVP, peripheral oedema, S3 gallop, bibasal crackles
  - Hypotension ± narrow pulse pressure (suggests low output state)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Acute coronary syndrome (MI)** – regional ECG changes, reciprocal depression, risk factors
  - **Pericarditis** – chest pain but no myocardial involvement (normal troponin, echo normal)
  - **Takotsubo cardiomyopathy** – post-menopausal women, emotional stress, apical ballooning on echo
  - **Viral respiratory infection** – no cardiac signs, troponin normal
  - **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, D-dimer/cTPA needed
- **Splitter:** The **ECG** – diffuse ST elevation (myocarditis/pericarditis) vs regional ST elevation with reciprocal changes (MI). Also, **echo** – segmental wall motion abnormality (MI) vs global dysfunction (myocarditis).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - 12-lead ECG (immediate) – look for diffuse ST elevation, PR depression, low voltage, electrical alternans (tamponade)
  - Capillary blood gas (lactate, pH if shock)
  - Focused echo (if available) – assess LV function, effusion, tamponade
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, troponin (high-sensitivity – often elevated but rises slower than MI)
  - D-dimer (if PE suspected)
  - Viral serology (later – EBV, CMV, parvovirus B19, COVID-19) – not urgent in first hour
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (if heart failure suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First line:** Chest X-ray (cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, effusions)
  - **Second line:** Transthoracic echo (urgent if haemodynamic instability or diagnostic uncertainty) – assess LV size, function, wall motion, effusion
  - **If stable:** Cardiac MRI (with T2-STIR and LGE) – gold standard for non-invasive diagnosis
- **Special tests:**
  - Endomyocardial biopsy (only if unexplained heart failure, giant cell/tuberculosis/sarcoid suspected)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** The **Lake Louise Criteria** (MRI-based – T2 oedema + LGE). Not for acute ward use, but helpful for cardiology referral.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG + troponin** in any young adult with chest pain – missing MI delays reperfusion.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stabilise airway/breathing/circulation.** If shock: IV fluids (but avoid overload if LV dysfunction), inotropes (e.g., dobutamine) – consult ICU
  - **Treat arrhythmias** – if haemodynamically unstable, DC cardioversion; if stable, consider amiodarone (avoid class I agents in acute myocarditis)
  - **Pain control:** Paracetamol or NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) if no contraindications (caution with heart failure/renal impairment). Colchicine can be added for pericarditis component.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Heart failure management** – if LVEF <40%: start ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril) + beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol) after haemodynamic stabilisation
  - **Monitor** – continuous ECG telemetry (risk of ventricular arrhythmias)
  - **Serial echocardiograms** – every 24–48h if unstable
  - **Hydration** – keep euvolemic; avoid excessive fluids
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Restrict physical activity** for at least 3–6 months (risk of sudden cardiac death with exercise)
  - **Follow-up echo** at 3–6 months to assess recovery
  - **Immunosuppression** (e.g., prednisolone) only for specific causes (giant cell, sarcoid, fungal) – discuss with cardiology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 12h if euvolemic (watch for overload). If cardiogenic shock, restrict to 20–30 mL/kg.
  - VTE prophylaxis: Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin) if not bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal (avoid high sodium if heart failure)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (including BP, HR, SpO2, temperature); if telemetry, document any arrhythmia

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** 1 g PO/IV QDS PRN pain (max 4 g/day)
  - **Ibuprofen** 400–600 mg PO TDS (if no heart failure/renal impairment) – max 2.4 g/day
  - **Colchicine** 500 µg PO BD for 3 months (if pericarditis component) – check renal function (reduce if eGFR <30)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Dobutamine** – for cardiogenic shock: start at 2.5 µg/kg/min, titrate to effect (but only in ICU/HDU setting)
  - **Amiodarone** – for VT: 300 mg IV over 20–60 min (contraindicated in hyperthyroidism/long QT)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs – avoid in renal impairment, GI bleed, heart failure
  - Colchicine – reduces clearance if on clarithromycin, statins, cyclosporine; check CYP3A4
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Colchicine: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (500 µg alternate days) – avoid if on dialysis
  - Ramipril: start at 1.25 mg OD if eGFR <30, up-titrate slowly
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **NSAIDs given to patient with undiagnosed heart failure or acute kidney injury** – can precipitate oliguric renal failure or pulmonary oedema. Always check creatinine and clinical volume status first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cardiogenic shock (5–10%)
  - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT/VF) – can be fatal
  - Heart block (especially giant cell or sarcoid)
  - Pericardial effusion / tamponade
- **Chronic:**
  - Dilated cardiomyopathy (persistent LV dysfunction)
  - Chronic heart failure
  - Recurrent myocarditis
  - Sudden cardiac death (rare if full recovery)

### Prognosis
- Most patients recover fully with supportive care (70–80%)
- Mortality <5% (higher if viral/staphylococcal or giant cell)
- Chronic heart failure develops in ~20% – require lifelong monitoring and medication

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO** a **bedside echo** (even limited) within the first hour – look for LV function, effusion, tamponade
- **DO** **start telemetry** immediately – the first 48h are highest risk for arrhythmia
- **DO** **tell the patient to avoid exercise** for 3–6 months; even light exertion can precipitate arrhythmia or worsen inflammation

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NOT** assuming young age = myocarditis – always rule out MI with ECG and troponin
- **NOT** giving **NSAIDs** without checking renal function and heart failure status
- **NOT** discharging without arranging **cardiology follow-up and echo** – recovery may be incomplete and needs monitoring

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Chest pain worsens or becomes unrelieved by simple analgesia
  - New shortness of breath, palpitations, syncope, or dizzy spells
  - Fever returns with worsening malaise
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology outpatient review within 2–4 weeks
  - Repeat echo at 6 weeks and 6 months
  - Advise to inform GP and avoid driving if arrhythmia history (DVLA rules)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or shock (systolic BP <90, lactate >2 mmol/L)
  - ECG shows VT/VF or high-grade AV block (Mobitz II/complete heart block)
  - Echo shows severe LV dysfunction (LVEF <35%) or tamponade
  - New arrhythmia causing haemodynamic instability
- **Crash team:** if any of the above with loss of consciousness or respiratory arrest
